---
title: "Background"
---

This site is a thin layer over data curated by the
[Journal Checker Tool](https://journalcheckertool.org/) (JCT). The link between an
agreement, the institutions taking part, and the journals it covers is the
product of a voluntary, partly manual process — not an authoritative feed from
publishers — so it helps to know how that data is assembled.

## How the data is assembled

1. A publisher and a library consortium (or a single institution) negotiate a
   [transformative agreement](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/).
2. The consortium or institution registers it in the
   [ESAC Transformative Agreement Registry](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/agreement-registry/).
   Sharing the terms is
   [voluntary](https://esac-initiative.org/about/transformative-agreements/share/),
   and agreements are registered at the consortium level rather than per member.
3. The JCT data team crawls ESAC regularly, then contacts the consortium directly
   to obtain the covered titles and the institutions that have access — this list
   is curated by hand, not scraped (Hope 2020).
4. The JCT publishes the result as CC0
   ["Transformative Agreements Public Data"](https://journalcheckertool.org/transformative-agreements/)
   and an [API](https://journalcheckertool.org/apidocs).
5. This site consumes that data and matches agreements to TU9 universities by
   ROR identifier.

A single agreement can give different institutions access to different sets of
titles, which the JCT records in its own per-agreement sheets; there is
no fixed one-to-one relationship between an agreement and its journal and
institution lists (Kiley 2023).

## How this site matches agreements

An agreement is listed for a university when one of the university's ROR
identifiers appears among the agreement's participating institutions. A
university is represented by its own ROR ids, including its library where the
library has a separate ROR — for example Dresden also counts agreements held by
the [SLUB](https://ror.org/03wf51b65) and Hannover those held by the
[TIB](https://ror.org/04aj4c181). University clinics are not counted; in this
data they only appear on agreements where the university itself is already
listed, so they add no coverage.

## What the dates and identifiers mean

Curation happens in periodic review cycles, not as a live feed, so the dates are
**observational**: the JCT records when it first and last observed a journal or
institution to be part of an agreement, not when it actually joined or left.
Journals are identified by print and online ISSN, institutions by
[ROR](https://ror.org/) where available.

## Caveats

The figures here are a conservative lower bound: independent checks of this kind
of JCT-based matching find it accurate but incomplete — high precision, lower
recall (de Jonge et al. 2025); a related JCT-based analysis was likewise designed
to underestimate rather than overestimate the open access it counts (Jahn 2025).
Only agreements that have been fully reviewed and confirmed Plan S–compliant
(JCT 2026b) appear in the public index, an expired agreement is dropped about
three months after it ends (JCT 2026a), and coverage depends on consortia
choosing to register and to respond to the JCT's requests — so this is a
curated snapshot, not a complete or historical record.
The agreements also cover open access only for articles where the TU9 author is
the corresponding author (the `ca_only` field in the agreements CSV).

## References

- Hope, H. (2020). Enabling accurate results within the Journal Checker Tool.
  *sOApbox, official blog of cOAlition S*, 18 November 2020.
  <https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/enabling-accurate-results-within-the-journal-checker-tool/>
- Kiley, R. (2023). Introducing new search features in the Journal Checker Tool
  for Transformative Agreements. *sOApbox, official blog of cOAlition S*,
  22 September 2023.
  <https://www.coalition-s.org/blog/introducing-new-search-features-in-the-journal-checker-tool-for-transformative-agreements/>
- de Jonge, H., Kramer, B., & Sondervan, J. (2025). Tracking transformative
  agreements through open metadata: Method and validation using Dutch Research
  Council NWO funded papers. *Quantitative Science Studies*, 6, 1215–1227.
  <https://doi.org/10.1162/qss.a.24>
- Jahn, N. (2025). How open are hybrid journals included in transformative
  agreements? *Quantitative Science Studies*, 6, 242–262.
  <https://doi.org/10.1162/qss_a_00348>
- Journal Checker Tool. (2026a). What happens to "expired" Transformative
  Agreements? *FAQ*.
  <https://journalcheckertool.org/faq/#what-happens-to-expired-transformative-agreements>
- Journal Checker Tool. (2026b). What is the Journal Checker Tool for? *FAQ*.
  <https://journalcheckertool.org/faq/#what-is-the-journal-checker-tool-for>
